coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots and photovoltaic applications
coredata.dc:creator: Lim, Hwain
Liu, Yang
Kim, Hak Yong
Son, Dong Ick
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we report the synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots that were easily obtained from citric acid and ethanediamine. The synthesized carbon quantum dots (QDs) had various wavelengths and improved electron extraction. We fabricated photovoltaic cells based on these QDs-integrated in polymer nanocomposite used as an interlayer (electron extraction layer) consisting of carbon QDs dispersed by spin coating on an ethoxylated polyethylenimine. In addition, we investigated the photovoltaic performance of the photovoltaic cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Nanocomposite structures
Inverted polymer solar cells
Photovoltaic performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the energy level of carbon dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: D. I. Son appreciates the financial support from the KIST Institution Program. This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT ( NRF-2017R1A2B3002307 ). Also, this research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT ( 2016M3A7B4900135 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the synthesis method of the CQDs. (b) TEM image of the CQDs. The insert is an enlarged TEM image of the CQDs. (c) XRD patterns of the CQDs.
(a) AFM image of the CQDs on the Si substrate, 1.5 × 1.5 μm. (b) Diameter distribution from the analysis of the AFM images. (c) Measured height profile along the white line in (a).
(a) C 1s (the insert is the FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs), (b) O 1s and (c) N 1s XPS spectra of the CQDs.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry of the CQDs at a scan rate of 100 mV/s. (b) Device structures of the inverted polymer solar cells (iPSCs) with the CQD interlayer. (c) Energy band diagram of the inverted solar cells with the CQD interlayer.
(a) The J-V characteristics of devices under AM 1.5G illumination. (b) EQE spectra of devices with the CQD interlayer.
Device performance of inverted polymer solar cells with CQDs as an interlayer.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots and photovoltaic applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Composite Materials, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, 92 Chudong-ro, Bongdong-eup, Wanju-gun, Jeonbuk 55324, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of BIN Convergence technology, Chonbuk National University, 567 Baekje-daero, Deokjin-gu, Jeonju, Jeonbuk 54896, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we report the synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots that were easily obtained from citric acid and ethanediamine. The synthesized carbon quantum dots (QDs) had various wavelengths and improved electron extraction. We fabricated photovoltaic cells based on these QDs-integrated in polymer nanocomposite used as an interlayer (electron extraction layer) consisting of carbon QDs dispersed by spin coating on an ethoxylated polyethylenimine. In addition, we investigated the photovoltaic performance of the photovoltaic cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, solution-processing technology has been widely employed to fabricate bistable memory devices [1], field-effect transistors [2], light-emitting diodes [3], and photovoltaic (PV) cells [4] because of their potential applications in next-generation electronic and optoelectronic devices. This technology promises good results in developing low cost, efficient, bright, and large area color displays that are compatible with flexible and bendable substrates. Solution-based nanomaterials have been widely studied as promising opto-electronic materials. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a member of opto-electronic nanomaterials, are of increasing importance because they have better properties compared to inorganic quantum dots: They are economical, and they have low toxicity, excellent water dispersibility, and satisfactory biocompatibility. From these outstanding merits, CQDs have proved to be suitable for bioimaging, chemical and optical sensing, photocatalysis (or photo-electrochemical cells), fast energy conversion and so on. [5] These days, CQDs are becoming more attractive materials for many applications because of their potential in next-generation electronic and optoelectronic devices, especially due to their excellent physicochemical stabilities, multi-functionalities, and facile functionalization. Thus, nanocomposites structures, including nanoparticles, have emerged as excellent candidates for promising applications in electronic and optoelectronic devices operating at lower currents, low power consumption, low voltage and higher temperatures [6–12].

In this study, we report the synthesis of CQDs that were easily obtained from citric acid and ethanediamine (EDA). In addition, our characterization of the CQDs investigated the optical, electrical and structural properties with various techniques. To apply the characteristics of CQDs, we fabricated PV cells based on CQD/polyethylenimine ethoxylated (PEIE) composites as an interfacial layer with an electron extraction property on an indium‑tin-oxide (ITO)/glass substrate. In addition, we investigated the mechanism and performance of the PV cells.

2.1 g citric acid was slowly added to 30 mL of a distilled water solution containing 1.8 g ethylenediamine. After the clear solution was stirred, it underwent microwave treatment for 8 min in a 750 W microwave oven. Then the resultant product was dissolved in deionized water and centrifuged for 30 min at 5000 rpm before 24 h of dialysis in a disposable biodialyzer (1 kDa molecular weight cut-off, purchased from Millipore-Sigma). The final dialyzed dispersion of CQDs was obtained and freeze-dried for further use.

The morphology and size distribution of the CQDs were determined via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips Tecnai G2 F20 microscope, Netherlands) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Park Systems NX10). X-ray diffractometry (XRD) patterns were recorded using Cu Kα (λ = 1.540 Å) radiation (max-2500, Rigaku). The chemical structure of the CQDs was measured with a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Frontier, Perkin-Elmer). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of the CQDs were measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600) (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-alpha) was performed using monochromated Al Kα X-ray photons (hν = 1486.6 eV).

The QY of prepared CQDs was measured by comparing that of the reference quinine sulfate (54% in 0.1 M H2SO4). The QY was calculated as follows:
Q x
=
Q st
I x
/
A x
A st
/
I st
η x
/
η st
2
where st means the standard reference, x is the sample for test. Q is the quantum yield, and I and A are the integrated emission intensity and optical absorption of the measured sample, respectively. η is the refractive index.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the energy level of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical cyclic voltammetry was conducted on a ZIVE SP2 electrochemical workstation. The CQDs were dispersed in a 0.5% Nafion solution of isopropanol and dimethylformamide. The solution was dropped on glassy carbon, which was used as the working electrode. A platinum wire and an Ag/AgNO3 electrode in 0.01 M MeCN acetonitrile (MeCN) served as the counter and reference electrode, respectively. A solution of 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium phosphorus hexafluoride (TBAPF6) in anhydrous acetonitrile was used as the electrolyte. Measurement was done at potential scan rates of 100 mV/s and 50 mV/s at room temperature.

Before making the PV devices, we prepared colloidal CQDs. The concentration of the CQDs solutions was formed by dissolving CQDs (50 wt%) in ethanol in the proper proportions. The ITO glass substrates were cleaned with acetone, ethanol and isopropanol, respectively. The ITO glass was then further treated with O2 plasma for 40 s. the PEIE layer was coated on the ITO glass at 4000 rpm for 40 s by spin-coating and then annealed with substrates at 110 °C for 10 min. Then the substrates were transferred into a N2-filled glove box. Immediately afterwards, the CQD solution was spin-coated onto PEIE film substrates at 2000 rpm for 40 s and then the PTB7:PC71BM solution was spin-coated onto the CQDs/PEIE film substrates at 1400 rpm for 10 s. Finally, MoO3 (10 nm) and Ag (80 nm) were sequentially deposited by thermal evaporation. The substrates were finally annealed at 60 °C for the proper times before use. The solar cells were then tested in air under an AM 1.5G illumination of 100 mW cm−2 (Oriel 1 kW solar simulator), which was calibrated with the International System of Units (SI) (SRC-1000-TC-KG5-N, VLSI Standards, Inc.) for accurate measurement. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) was measured using an Oriel IQE-200 (Newport), a calibrated Si UV detector, and an SR8570 low noise current amplifier.

The chemical synthesis process of CQDs is easily accomplished with citric acid and ethanediamine (EDA) by microwave treatment and further purification, as illustrated in Fig. 1(a). EDA with the amino group (NH2+) is a pharmaceutical ingredient commonly used in large quantities to produce many commercial chemicals. We chose the EDA not only as a root, but it also acted as the N source for the N-doping process to develop photoluminescence (PL) for N-type doped CQDs [13]. EDA passivated CQD solutions exhibit a bright cyan color when irradiated by a UV light source with 365 nm. In addition, the amino groups were attached to the surface of the CQDs and performed a crucial role in light emission with a band-gap shift from blue to green according to various excitation sources [14]; they also improve the electron extraction because they have excellent quenching characteristics [15]. The QY of the CQDs was calculated to be 9.84% using quinine sulfate. These results suggest that a large percentage of sunlight can be effectively utilized because of the luminescent down–shift effect [16].

The TEM image in Fig. 1(b) shows that the average diameter of CQDs is approximately 10 nm from the morphology and size distribution of the as-prepared CQDs. To determine the composition and structure of the as-prepared CQDs, XRD and FT-IR spectroscopy were used to characterize a dry solid CQDs sample. The XRD patterns of CQDs are shown in Fig. 1(c). A broad diffraction peak centred at 2θ = 23.7° is observed and is ascribed to the (002) lattice spacing of a graphitic-like structure.

In addition, we investigated the morphology of the CQDs using AFM tool. The results by AFM indicated that the CQDs (Fig. 2 ) were evenly distributed on the Si substrate. The typical topological AFM image is shown in Fig. 2(a). The statistics based on 159 CQDs in the AFM image suggest that the height distribution of the CQDs was centered at ~5 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The diameter of the CQDs was in the range of 3–10 nm. We statistically evaluated 151 objects; the average diameter of the CQDs was 5.5 nm. The AFM images further confirm the uniformity of the CQDs and indicate that the average thickness of the CQDs was below 3.5 nm (Fig. 2(c)).

The elemental composition of the CQDs was characterized by FT-IR and XPS. The FT-IR result clearly displayed several characteristic peaks. We further analyzed the chemical composition of the CQDs using XPS equipment. Four distinct carbon states in the C 1s spectrum were observed at 285.8, 286.9, 288.2 and 289.3 eV, which are relevant to CC, CN, CO, and CN/CO, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3(a) [17]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3(b)) has two peaks about 531.7 and 532.9 eV, which correspond to CO and COH/COC bands [18,19]. The N 1s spectrum revealed three peaks at 400.7, 400.2 and 339.4 eV as shown in Fig. 3(c), which correspond to the CNC, N(C)3, and NH/NH2 [18]. Through a hydrothermal treatment, the CQDs underwent a condensation and dehydration reaction process between the carboxyl and amino groups. Therefore, the surface functional groups of the CQDs were confirmed from FT-IR and XPS results. The NH and CO bonds observed by XPS were consistent with amide bond formation in the FT-IR spectrum.

To precisely clarify the energy level of the CQDs in inverted polymer solar cell structures, we used cyclic voltammetry (CV) to measure the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) level and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level of the CQDs. The cyclic voltammetry of the CQD solution in Nafion at a scan rate of 100 mV/s are shown in Fig. 4(a). The HOMO and LUMO levels were calculated from the onset oxidation potential (E′ox) and onset reduction potential (E′red). The HOMO level of the CQDs was calculated as −6.541 eV according to the following equation: EHOMO = −IP = −(E′ox + 4.741) (eV). The LUMO level of the CQDs was calculated as −4.04 eV according to the following equation: ELUMO = −Ea = −(E′red + 4.741) (eV) [19].
To confirm the electron extraction characteristics of the CQDs, we fabricated photovoltaic cells based the CQDs/PEIE composites as an interfacial layer with an electron extraction property.

The solar cell device was investigated as follows. The CV measurement of the HOMO level and the LUMO level of the CQDs revealed the device structure and energy level of inverted polymer solar cells with CQDs as an interfacial layer with electron extraction property as shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c). The energy levels lying in the inverted polymer solar cells (iPSCs) demonstrate the possible pathways of current generation and transportation after the excitation of the photoactive materials (PTB7, PC71BM and CQDs), as shown in Fig. 4(c).

The work function of the ITO layer after PEIE modification decreased from 4.7 eV to 3.08 eV, which was calculated using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). The decrease can efficiently promote electron transportation in the layer [20]. In this structure, the PEIE layer acts as a surface modifying layer, which plays a role as an interface control on the ITO layer for good electron selectivity.
The HOMO and LUMO levels of the CQDs are located below the corresponding HOMOs and LUMOs of PTB7 and PC71BM, indicating that the photo-induced charge transfer is systemically favorable to both sides.

To verify the effect of CQDs as an interfacial layer with an electron extraction property on photovoltaic cells performance, we fabricated inverted architecture BHJ PSCs with an active blend layer of PTB7 and PC71BM. The current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics of the devices under AM 1.5G illumination are shown in Fig. 5(a), which shows that the control device (with PTB7: PC71BM) has a lower JSC than photovoltaic devices fabricated using a solution of dissolved CQDs (50 wt%) in ethanol. After the CQDs as the electron extraction layer were inserted, the performance of JSC generally improved. More details of the performance are summarized in Table 1 . The control device with PTB7:PC71BM demonstrates an open circuit voltage (VOC) of 0.76 V, a short circuit current density (JSC) of 14.4 mA/cm2, a fill factor (FF) of 71.7% and a resulting power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 7.90%. When the CQDs are located below the PTB7:PC71BM bulk heterojunction polymer composite absorption layer, the performance is improved remarkably and the device with CQDs shows the highest PCE. The device with CQDs exhibits a VOC of 0.76 V, JSC of 16.7 mA/cm2, FF of 65.5% and PCE of 8.34%. The factor that accounts for the improved PCE is the increases in JSC and PCE. In the device with CQDs, the JSC increased about 16% and Voc increased about 2.96% more than in the control devices. Consequently, the PCE increased about 12.2% compared with the control devices. This result indicates that CQDs can enhance the charge transport properties and surface morphology. The enhancement of JSC is related to the external quantum efficiency (EQE). Fig. 5(b) shows the EQE spectra of inverted polymer solar cells with CQDs as the electron extraction layer. Compared with the control device, the overall EQE data shows enhancement, which means that CQDs improve the charge collection properties. Under AM 1.5G illumination, absorbed photons have separated electrons and holes and then charge carriers transfer at the electrode. The CQD interlayer below the PTB7:PC71BM bulk heterojunction polymer composites absorption layer creates efficient charge separation and prevents charge recombination. In the device with CQDs, the EQE is significantly higher than in the control device in the ultra violet (UV) and visible range (300–780 nm). The device with the CQDs reaches a maximum value of 60.9%, while control device is 46.8% in the 440 nm wavelength. It is enhanced almost 30.1% around the 440 nm range.

In conclusion, we demonstrated a simple and facile technique for synthesizing CQDs using a simple chemical method and investigated the structural, optical and electrical properties of the CQDs. To confirm the electron extraction characteristics of the CQDs, we fabricated photovoltaic cells based the CQDs as an interfacial layer with an electron extraction property. The photovoltaic performance of iPSCs was due to the fact that the CQDs below the active layer considerably improved the charge transport properties, which further enhanced JSC and FF. As a result, the iPSCs with CQDs as an interlayer was enhanced more than 12.2%, which was better than the control devices in PCE. The highest power conversion efficiencies of the iPSCs with the CQDs as the extraction layer were enhanced by up to 8.34%. The EQE was significantly higher than the control device in the UV and visible range. In particular, it was improved by almost 30.1% around the 440 nm range.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: D. I. Son appreciates the financial support from the KIST Institution Program. This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (NRF-2017R1A2B3002307). Also, this research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (2016M3A7B4900135).
